solutions.solution: Vendors are not meant for footpaths. So, they should be driven away from there. This is what I suggests. But will it not be an extreme action? Yes, it will be. Therefore earn their livelihood from that place and this action will deprive them of livelihood. This is what II suggests. But, will it resolve the problem? But if footpaths are meant for increasing walk, then where will they stay? Therefore, the best action would be to provide alternative place for them so that they can carn their bread and so use force to drive them from that place footpaths are cleared. Hence, both I and II follow.

The problem is lose-making by Vaydoot. Will I solve the problem? Reducing wasteful expenditure and increasing fare would definitely reduce loss. Will II solve the problem? Yes (it is obvious). But is it a suitable action? Yes, I is; but II isn't. To reduce wasteful expenditure a systematic alternative step. But it is not a wise thing to invest huge amount in a loss-making unit. Vaydoot should be made to stand on its own leg, and not to walk on others' legs. Hence, I follows but II does not.

First, if Indian companies face stiff competition, it would be advisable for other markets as well. This would be a practical approach that looks 'no better' to a buyer. Secondly, there is nothing wrong with searching for other markets as well. This would be a practical approach. In case Japanese companies drive them out of West European markets, Indian companies may fall back on these alternative markets. Hence, I and II both follow.

To improve the unhygienic condition is always a welcome step and I view of the statement, it becomes even more necessary. Hence, I unquestionably follows. But II does not follow. We should not medicate any child unless he has actually contracted the disease. Prevention is all right but curing should begin only after the disease has been contracted.

If sale has gone down there must be some solid reasons. The company must know these reasons. Since I suggests a similar thing, it follows. But II does not follow. The company should first know if price was a factor behind the drop in sales. Without knowing this, reducing price may turn out to be a harmful action.

If such a serious allegation has been levelled, it must be thoroughly examined. So, I follows. But II is an arbitrary action. If officials of the state police can be corrupt, so can be the officials of the Central government. The issue is corruption and mere transfer of control will not solve this problem.

It is an absurd suggestion; when one industry is facing trouble because of regulations, it talks of regulating the supply of other industrial raw materials too! It is a better action because it would tend to solve the problem.
